title: Tarot Noblet
description: Produced around 1650 by Jean Noblet, this deck is the earliest known example of the Tarot de Marseille. Preserving the unaltered symbolism of the 17th century, it offers a rare glimpse into the medieval roots of tarot, influenced by Gothic art and traditional European iconography. Divided into Major and Minor Arcana the Noblet deck stands as both a functional divination tool and a vital piece of tarot history.

title: Visconti di Modrone
description: The Visconti deck, commissioned around 1466 by the Visconti family and attributed to Bonifacio Bembo, stands among the oldest surviving tarot decks. Hand-painted and adorned with gold and silver leaf, it was designed as a luxury courtly object rather than for mass production. With 67 surviving cards, the deck reveals the earliest evolution of tarot imagery, including expanded court figures — male and female Knights and Pages – and the rare inclusion of the theological virtues among the trumps (Faith, Hope, Charity).

title: Pierpont-Morgan Bergamo Tarot
description: Created shortly after 1450, the Visconti-Sforza Tarot is among the oldest surviving decks from the era when tarot was played at court. Today, its remaining cards are housed at the Accademia Carrara and The Morgan Library & Museum. Attributed primarily to Bonifacio Bembo and Francesco Zavattari, the deck features richly dressed noble figures – likely inspired by the Visconti and Sforza courts – set against luminous gold and decorative backgrounds. Three replacement trumps were later painted by Antonio Cicognara.

title: Rider-Waite-Smith Tarot
description: The Rider-Waite-Smith deck is a foundational 1910 masterpiece of occult art, created by Arthur Edward Waite and artist Pamela Colman Smith. It revolutionized tarot by replacing traditional "pip" markers with evocative, full pictorial scenes for all 78 cards, facilitating intuitive interpretation. Today, it remains the world’s most influential deck, with its iconic visual language serving as the primary standard for modern tarot iconography.

title: Sola Busca Tarot
description: The Sola Busca Tarot is the only complete 78-card deck surviving from the 15th century, notable for being the first to feature fully illustrated, scenic Minor Arcana. Attributed to Nicola di Maestro Antonio d’Ancona (c. 1491), the deck replaces traditional iconography with classical heroes and complex hermetic-alchemical symbolism. Now held at the Pinacoteca di Brera, it serves as the foundational precursor to the modern Rider-Waite-Smith system.

title: Tarocchi Brambilla
description: Created in the mid-15th century and attributed to the painter Bonifacio Bembo, the Tarocchi Brambilla is one of the earliest surviving Italian tarot decks. Likely commissioned for the court of Milan under Francesco Sforza, it reflects the artistic culture and heraldic symbolism of the Visconti–Sforza dynasty. Originally composed of 68 cards, only 48 survive today, preserved in the Pinacoteca di Brera in Milan. Hand-painted on pressed cardboard and richly embellished with gold and silver leaf, the cards were crafted as luxury objects rather than printed playing cards.
